title: Quiz: QAOA for Combinatorial Optimization
description: Test your understanding of QAOA including problem encoding, mixer design, parameter optimization, and applications to Max-Cut and portfolio optimization

questions.question: What is the initial state typically used in QAOA?

options.text: |0>^n - all qubits in the zero state
|+>^n - equal superposition of all computational basis states
A random quantum state
The ground state of the cost Hamiltonian

questions.explanation: QAOA begins with |+>^n = H^n|0>^n, which is an equal superposition of all computational basis states. This ensures the algorithm starts with equal probability of all possible solutions, allowing the variational optimization to find the best one.

questions.question: In the context of QAOA, what does the parameter p represent?

options.text: The number of qubits in the circuit
The probability of finding the optimal solution
The number of alternating cost/mixer layers
The precision of the measurement

questions.explanation: The parameter p represents the circuit depth in terms of alternating layers. Each layer consists of a cost Hamiltonian evolution (parameterized by gamma) followed by a mixer evolution (parameterized by beta). Higher p generally gives better approximations but increases circuit depth.

questions.question: For the Max-Cut problem, how is the cost Hamiltonian structured?

options.text: Sum of X operators on all qubits
Sum of ZZ terms for each edge in the graph
Single Z operator on the first qubit
Product of all Z operators

questions.explanation: The Max-Cut cost Hamiltonian is C = sum_{(i,j) in E} (1 - Z_i Z_j)/2. Each edge contributes a ZZ term that evaluates to +1 when the connected vertices are in different partitions (cut edge) and -1 when they're in the same partition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the XY mixer instead of the standard X mixer?

options.text: It requires fewer quantum gates
It preserves the Hamming weight (number of 1s) of the state
It always finds the optimal solution
It eliminates the need for classical optimization

questions.explanation: The XY mixer (XX + YY terms) preserves the Hamming weight of bitstrings, making it suitable for problems with cardinality constraints like 'select exactly k items'. The standard X mixer can change the number of 1s in a bitstring.

questions.question: QUBO (Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization) problems can always be transformed into Ising Hamiltonians suitable for QAOA.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Any QUBO problem can be transformed to an Ising model using the substitution x_i = (1 - z_i)/2, where x_i is binary {0,1} and z_i is Ising {-1,+1}. This transformation preserves the optimization structure while expressing it in terms of Pauli Z operators.

questions.question: In portfolio optimization using QAOA, what does a binary variable x_i = 1 typically represent?

options.text: The exact amount to invest in asset i
Asset i is selected for the portfolio
Asset i has positive return
The correlation between asset i and the market

questions.explanation: In binary portfolio optimization, x_i = 1 means asset i is selected (included) in the portfolio, while x_i = 0 means it is not selected. This formulation is suitable for problems like 'select the best k assets' rather than continuous allocation.

questions.question: Which factors affect QAOA performance? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The number of layers p
The quality of parameter optimization
The choice of mixer operator
The color of the quantum computer

questions.explanation: QAOA performance depends on: (a) circuit depth p - more layers generally improve quality, (b) parameter optimization - finding good gamma/beta values is crucial, and (c) mixer design - different mixers are better for different problem structures. Physical properties like color are irrelevant.

questions.question: What is the approximation ratio for p=1 QAOA on Max-Cut for 3-regular graphs?

options.text: At least 0.5 (50%)
Exactly 1.0 (optimal)
Unknown - it varies randomly

questions.explanation: For 3-regular graphs, p=1 QAOA provably achieves an approximation ratio of at least 0.6924. This means the expected cut value is at least 69.24% of the maximum possible cut. This is a worst-case guarantee; average performance is typically better.
